1.texto_original: How hath the Lord covered the daughter of Zion with a cloud in his anger! He hath cast down from heaven unto the earth the beauty of Israel, And hath not remembered his footstool in the day of his anger.
1.normal:
Devotional Study of Lamentations 2:1

The first verse establishes the foundation of all biblical revelation: God as sovereign Creator. The phrase "In the beginning" places us at the inaugural moment of creation, not as an event within time, but as the beginning of time itself. God is not part of creation, but its absolute origin.

The verb "created" (from the Hebrew bara) is exclusive to God in the Scriptures, indicating creation ex nihilo (from nothing). This is not about transforming pre-existing matter, but calling into existence what did not exist before. The expression "the heavens and the earth" encompasses the totality of created reality.

This verse answers fundamental questions: Where do we come from? Who is in control? The answer is clear: everything comes from God and depends on Him. As believers, this truth gives us security in divine sovereignty over our lives.

"How hath the Lord covered the daughter of Zion with a cloud in his anger! He hath cast down from heaven unto the earth the beauty of Israel, And hath not remembered his footstool in the day of his anger."
1.academico:
Exegetical Analysis of Lamentations 2:1

Philological Analysis

The original Hebrew text presents crucial elements for exegesis. Bereshit (בְּרֵאשִׁית) is a prepositional construction meaning "in the beginning of", indicating the absolute beginning. The verb bara (בָּרָא) appears in perfect tense (qatal), denoting completed action. This verb has God as its exclusive subject in the Tanakh, distinguishing it from asah (to make) and yatsar (to form).

Historical Context

Source criticism identifies this opening with the Priestly tradition (P), dated to the exilic or post-exilic period (6th-5th century BCE). This contrasts with Mesopotamian creation narratives such as the Enuma Elish (18th century BCE), where creation results from divine conflict.

Theological Implications

The theology of creation establishes: (1) Radical monotheism; (2) Creation ex nihilo; (3) Intrinsic goodness of creation; (4) Absolute sovereignty without cosmic opposition.

Archaeological Evidence

Discoveries at Ugarit (Ras Shamra) and Ebla illuminate Canaanite cosmogonic language, showing how Genesis employs common terms but strips them of mythological connotations.

"How hath the Lord covered the daughter of Zion with a cloud in his anger! He hath cast down from heaven unto the earth the beauty of Israel, And hath not remembered his footstool in the day of his anger."
1.ninos:
🌌 The Great Beginning! 🌍

Hello, universe explorers! 🚀 Imagine: before there was anything... not even time! ⏰... God decided to CREATE EVERYTHING! ✨

With a powerful "LET THERE BE!" 🤓, God made appear from absolute nothing ✨ the blue skies full of twinkling stars ⭐ and the Earth with mountains 🏔️, rivers 🌊 and animals 🐶. Like the greatest magic ever! 🪄

God is the most amazing artist 🎨 and everything is His masterpiece. Including YOU! 😊 When you see the clouds ☁️ or play in the park 🌳, remember: it all started with Gods creative love! ❤️

"How hath the Lord covered the daughter of Zion with a cloud in his anger! He hath cast down from heaven unto the earth the beauty of Israel, And hath not remembered his footstool in the day of his anger."

2.texto_original: The Lord hath swallowed up all the habitations of Jacob, and hath not pitied: He hath thrown down in his wrath the strongholds of the daughter of Judah; He hath brought them down to the ground; he hath profaned the kingdom and the princes thereof.

10.texto_original: The elders of the daughter of Zion sit upon the ground, they keep silence; They have cast up dust upon their heads; They have girded themselves with sackcloth: The virgins of Jerusalem hang down their heads to the ground.
10.normal:
Devotional Study of Lamentations 2:10

The first verse establishes the foundation of all biblical revelation: God as sovereign Creator. The phrase "In the beginning" places us at the inaugural moment of creation, not as an event within time, but as the beginning of time itself. God is not part of creation, but its absolute origin.

The verb "created" (from the Hebrew bara) is exclusive to God in the Scriptures, indicating creation ex nihilo (from nothing). This is not about transforming pre-existing matter, but calling into existence what did not exist before. The expression "the heavens and the earth" encompasses the totality of created reality.

This verse answers fundamental questions: Where do we come from? Who is in control? The answer is clear: everything comes from God and depends on Him. As believers, this truth gives us security in divine sovereignty over our lives.

"The elders of the daughter of Zion sit upon the ground, they keep silence; They have cast up dust upon their heads; They have girded themselves with sackcloth: The virgins of Jerusalem hang down their heads to the ground."
10.academico:
Exegetical Analysis of Lamentations 2:10

Philological Analysis

The original Hebrew text presents crucial elements for exegesis. Bereshit (בְּרֵאשִׁית) is a prepositional construction meaning "in the beginning of", indicating the absolute beginning. The verb bara (בָּרָא) appears in perfect tense (qatal), denoting completed action. This verb has God as its exclusive subject in the Tanakh, distinguishing it from asah (to make) and yatsar (to form).

Historical Context

Source criticism identifies this opening with the Priestly tradition (P), dated to the exilic or post-exilic period (6th-5th century BCE). This contrasts with Mesopotamian creation narratives such as the Enuma Elish (18th century BCE), where creation results from divine conflict.

Theological Implications

The theology of creation establishes: (1) Radical monotheism; (2) Creation ex nihilo; (3) Intrinsic goodness of creation; (4) Absolute sovereignty without cosmic opposition.

Archaeological Evidence

Discoveries at Ugarit (Ras Shamra) and Ebla illuminate Canaanite cosmogonic language, showing how Genesis employs common terms but strips them of mythological connotations.

"The elders of the daughter of Zion sit upon the ground, they keep silence; They have cast up dust upon their heads; They have girded themselves with sackcloth: The virgins of Jerusalem hang down their heads to the ground."
10.ninos:
🌌 The Great Beginning! 🌍

Hello, universe explorers! 🚀 Imagine: before there was anything... not even time! ⏰... God decided to CREATE EVERYTHING! ✨

With a powerful "LET THERE BE!" 🤓, God made appear from absolute nothing ✨ the blue skies full of twinkling stars ⭐ and the Earth with mountains 🏔️, rivers 🌊 and animals 🐶. Like the greatest magic ever! 🪄

God is the most amazing artist 🎨 and everything is His masterpiece. Including YOU! 😊 When you see the clouds ☁️ or play in the park 🌳, remember: it all started with Gods creative love! ❤️

"The elders of the daughter of Zion sit upon the ground, they keep silence; They have cast up dust upon their heads; They have girded themselves with sackcloth: The virgins of Jerusalem hang down their heads to the ground."

19.normal:
Devotional Study of Lamentations 2:19

The first verse establishes the foundation of all biblical revelation: God as sovereign Creator. The phrase "In the beginning" places us at the inaugural moment of creation, not as an event within time, but as the beginning of time itself. God is not part of creation, but its absolute origin.

The verb "created" (from the Hebrew bara) is exclusive to God in the Scriptures, indicating creation ex nihilo (from nothing). This is not about transforming pre-existing matter, but calling into existence what did not exist before. The expression "the heavens and the earth" encompasses the totality of created reality.

This verse answers fundamental questions: Where do we come from? Who is in control? The answer is clear: everything comes from God and depends on Him. As believers, this truth gives us security in divine sovereignty over our lives.

"Arise, cry out in the night, at the beginning of the watches; Pour out thy heart like water before the face of the Lord: Lift up thy hands toward him for the life of thy young children, that faint for hunger at the head of every street."
19.academico:
Exegetical Analysis of Lamentations 2:19

Philological Analysis

The original Hebrew text presents crucial elements for exegesis. Bereshit (בְּרֵאשִׁית) is a prepositional construction meaning "in the beginning of", indicating the absolute beginning. The verb bara (בָּרָא) appears in perfect tense (qatal), denoting completed action. This verb has God as its exclusive subject in the Tanakh, distinguishing it from asah (to make) and yatsar (to form).

Historical Context

Source criticism identifies this opening with the Priestly tradition (P), dated to the exilic or post-exilic period (6th-5th century BCE). This contrasts with Mesopotamian creation narratives such as the Enuma Elish (18th century BCE), where creation results from divine conflict.

Theological Implications

The theology of creation establishes: (1) Radical monotheism; (2) Creation ex nihilo; (3) Intrinsic goodness of creation; (4) Absolute sovereignty without cosmic opposition.

Archaeological Evidence

Discoveries at Ugarit (Ras Shamra) and Ebla illuminate Canaanite cosmogonic language, showing how Genesis employs common terms but strips them of mythological connotations.

"Arise, cry out in the night, at the beginning of the watches; Pour out thy heart like water before the face of the Lord: Lift up thy hands toward him for the life of thy young children, that faint for hunger at the head of every street."
19.ninos:
🌌 The Great Beginning! 🌍

Hello, universe explorers! 🚀 Imagine: before there was anything... not even time! ⏰... God decided to CREATE EVERYTHING! ✨

With a powerful "LET THERE BE!" 🤓, God made appear from absolute nothing ✨ the blue skies full of twinkling stars ⭐ and the Earth with mountains 🏔️, rivers 🌊 and animals 🐶. Like the greatest magic ever! 🪄

God is the most amazing artist 🎨 and everything is His masterpiece. Including YOU! 😊 When you see the clouds ☁️ or play in the park 🌳, remember: it all started with Gods creative love! ❤️

"Arise, cry out in the night, at the beginning of the watches; Pour out thy heart like water before the face of the Lord: Lift up thy hands toward him for the life of thy young children, that faint for hunger at the head of every street."

20.texto_original: See, O Jehovah, and behold to whom thou hast done thus! Shall the women eat their fruit, the children that are dandled in the hands? Shall the priest and the prophet be slain in the sanctuary of the Lord?

21.texto_original: The youth and the old man lie on the ground in the streets; My virgins and my young men are fallen by the sword: Thou hast slain them in the day of thine anger; Thou hast slaughtered, [and] not pitied.

22.texto_original: Thou hast called, as in the day of a solemn assembly, my terrors on every side; And there was none that escaped or remained in the day of Jehovah's anger: Those that I have dandled and brought up hath mine enemy consumed.
22.normal:
Devotional Study of Lamentations 2:22

The first verse establishes the foundation of all biblical revelation: God as sovereign Creator. The phrase "In the beginning" places us at the inaugural moment of creation, not as an event within time, but as the beginning of time itself. God is not part of creation, but its absolute origin.

The verb "created" (from the Hebrew bara) is exclusive to God in the Scriptures, indicating creation ex nihilo (from nothing). "Thou hast called, as in the day of a solemn assembly, my terrors on every side; And there was none that escaped or remained in the day of Jehovah's anger: Those that I have dandled and brought up hath mine enemy consumed."
